#start of clustering code
In [16]:
#encode teams
tmdf = df['Tm']

from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

le = LabelEncoder()

df['Tm'] = le.fit_transform(df['Tm'])

tmdf = le.transform(tmdf)

tmdf

In [20]:
#use elbow method to find optimal number of clusters

from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

wcss = []

for i in range(1, 11):
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters = i, init = 'k-means++', random_state = 42)
    kmeans.fit(clusterdf)
    wcss.append(kmeans.inertia_)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize = (10, 5))

sns.lineplot(range(1, 11), wcss, marker = 'o', color = 'red')

plt.title('The Elbow Method')

plt.xlabel('Number of Clusters')

plt.ylabel('WCSS')

plt.show()

C:\Users\dtgiv\anaconda3\lib\site-packages\sklearn\cluster\_kmeans.py:1036: UserWarning: KMeans is known to have a memory leak on Windows with MKL, when there are less chunks than available threads. You can avoid it by setting the environment variable OMP_NUM_THREADS=1.
  warnings.warn(
C:\Users\dtgiv\anaconda3\lib\site-packages\seaborn\_decorators.py:36: FutureWarning: Pass the following variables as keyword args: x, y. From version 0.12, the only valid positional argument will be `data`, and passing other arguments without an explicit keyword will result in an error or misinterpretation.
  warnings.warn(
In [21]:
#fit k-means to the data using 3 clusters and 300 iterations

kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters = 3, init = 'k-means++', max_iter = 300, n_init = 10, random_state = 0)

kmeans.fit(clusterdf)
Out[21]:
KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=0)
In [22]:
#visualize clustered data
sns.scatterplot(data = clusterdf, x = 'Tm', y = 'H', hue = kmeans.labels_)
Out[22]:
<AxesSubplot:xlabel='Tm', ylabel='H'>

#attach cluster labels to clusterdf
clusterdf['cluster'] = kmeans.labels_

print(clusterdf.to_string())

#view Cincinnati Reds players

In [37]:
#looking at the scatterplot above, I don't think 4 clusters makes sense for these data because the lowest numbers of hits
#are split into two clusters (0 & 2). However I think it is worth noting that the Reds (Tm 6) are the only team to have 2
#players clustered into the most elite group (1)
In [38]:

In [46]:
#correlation matrix
import seaborn as sns
sns.heatmap(X.corr(),
           annot = True,
           fmt = '.1g',
           center = 0,
           cmap = 'coolwarm',
           linewidths = .5,
           linecolor = 'black')
Out[46]:
<AxesSubplot:>
In [47]:
#some takeaways from the correlation matrix: it appears that there are strong correlations (0.7-1.0) among the following 
#variable pairs:
#runs and: hits; doubles; triples; home runs; stolen bases; walks; strikeouts
#hits and: doubles; home runs; walks; strikeouts
#doubles and: home runs, walks, strikeouts
#triples and: stolen bases
#home runs and: walks; strikeouts
#stolen bases and: strikeouts
In [58]:
#Principal Component Analysis
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
model = PCA(random_state = 1502).fit(X)
plt.plot(model.explained_variance_ratio_,
         linewidth = 4)
plt.xlabel('Components')
plt.ylabel('Explained Variance')
plt.show()
In [59]:
#Cumulative Graph
import numpy as np
plt.plot(np.cumsum(model.explained_variance_ratio_),
         linewidth = 4)
plt.xlabel('Components')
plt.ylabel('Explained Variance Cumulative')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
#PCA
model = PCA(n_components = 4,
            random_state = 1502).fit(X)

#Interpretation
model_interpretation = pd.DataFrame(model.components_,
                                    columns = X.columns)
model_interpretation

#interpretation:
#fields appearing to be greater than or equal to a threshold of 0.25:
#R (runs)
#H (hits)
#BB (walks)
#SO (strikeouts)
In [ ]:
#components
components = model.transform(X)
components = pd.DataFrame(components,
                          columns = ['Speed',
                                     'Base Running',
                                     'Frequency of Reaching Base',
                                     'Pitch Selection'])
components.head()
#X.head()
                                     
#merge with original dataset
#final_dataset = pd.concat([components, X], axis = 1)
#final_dataset
In [ ]:
In [ ]:

                         drop_first = True)

#dataset.head()
In [51]:
y = X.iloc[:, -1] #creates a dataset with only labels
x = X.iloc[:, :-1] #creates a dataset with everything else but the labels

y.head()
#y.info()

#split dataset into training and test set, 20% for testing and 80% for training - labels and data
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,
                                                    test_size = 0.2,
                                                    random_state = 1502)

y_test.describe()

In [54]:
#Random Forest
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators = 300,
                               criterion = "entropy",
                               random_state = 1502)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

predictions = model.predict(X_test)
predictions[:20]
Out[54]:
array([25, 24,  4, 12,  5, 18, 24, 19,  7, 27, 19,  6, 10,  3, 19,  2, 15,
        3, 19, 19])
In [55]:
#Confusion Matrix
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report
report = classification_report(y_test, predictions)
print(report)

#Feature Importance
importance = pd.Series(model.feature_importances_,
                       index = X_train.columns.values)
importance.nlargest(5).plot(kind = 'barh',
                            figsize = (12,8))
